perturbation.original_text: I will maintain patient privacy and protect and safeguard the c onfidentiality and security of PHI and FAU Confidential Information in accordance with Florida and federal laws and FAU policies and procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: I will endeavor to maintain patient privacy and protect and safeguard the confidentiality and security of PHI and FAU Confidential Information according to FAU policies and procedures.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the direct reference to compliance with 'Florida and federal laws' and weakening the commitment to 'endeavor', the contract now contradicts the mandatory compliance required by HIPAA and Florida state laws regarding patient privacy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA mandates strict adherence to privacy rules, not simply an 'endeavor' to comply. This modification suggests a weaker obligation than legally required.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I hereby agree as follows:'

Navigate to: The HIPAA Privacy Rule The HIPAA Privacy Rule establishes national standards to protect individuals' medical records and other individually identifiable health information (collectively defined as “protected health information”) and applies to health plans, health care clearinghouses, and those health care providers that conduct certain health care transactions electronically. The Rule requires appropriate safeguards to protect the privacy of protected health information and sets limits and conditions on the uses and disclosures that may be made of such information without an individual’s authorization. The Rule also gives individuals rights over their protected health information, including rights to examine and obtain a copy of their health records, to direct a covered entity to transmit to a third party an electronic copy of their protected health information in an electronic health record, and to request corrections. The Privacy Rule is located at 45 CFR Part 160 and Subparts A and E of Part 164 . Click here to view the combined regulation text of all HIPAA Administrative Simplification Regulations found at 45 CFR 160, 162, and 164. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with patient privacy laws. The original text states a direct commitment to maintain privacy 'in accordance with Florida and federal laws,' while the changed text only commits to 'endeavor to maintain' privacy according to 'FAU policies and procedures,' omitting the mandatory legal compliance. This creates a contradiction, as HIPAA (as described in Snippet 2) 'establishes national standards to protect individuals' medical records' and 'requires appropriate safeguards to protect the privacy of protected health information'. The changed text introduces a level of discretion ('endeavor') that undermines the strict adherence required by HIPAA, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: I understand that upon terminati on of my employment/affiliation /association with FAU, I will immediately return or destroy, as appropriate, any FAU Confiden tial Information, Restricted Data and Sensitive Data in my possession. I understand that my confi dentiality obligations under this Agreement will continue after the termination of this Agreement and after termination of my employment, affiliation or association with FAU.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of my employment/affiliation/association with FAU, I will return any FAU Confidential Information, Restricted Data, and Sensitive Data in my possession.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of the clause 'my confidentiality obligations under this Agreement will continue after the termination of this Agreement' creates a legal contradiction. Florida Statute 815.04 states that obligations to protect trade secrets and confidential information continue after termination of employment.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law explicitly protects trade secrets and confidential information even after employment ends. Removing the clause that states confidentiality obligations continue post-termination suggests an incorrect legal position.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I understand that upon terminati on of my employment/affiliation /association with FAU'

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XLVI > Chapter 815 > Section 04 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLVI CRIMES Chapter 815 COMPUTER-RELATED CRIMES Entire Chapter SECTION 04 Offenses against intellectual property. F.S. 815.04 815.04 Offenses against intellectual property. — (1) A person who willfully, knowingly, and without authorization introduces a computer contaminant or modifies or renders unavailable data, programs, or supporting documentation residing or existing internal or external to a computer, computer system, computer network, or electronic device commits an offense against intellectual property. (2) A person who willfully, knowingly, and without authorization destroys data, programs, or supporting documentation residing or existing internal or external to a computer, computer system, computer network, or electronic device commits an offense against intellectual property. (3) A person who willfully, knowingly, and without authorization discloses or takes data, programs, or supporting documentation that is a trade secret as defined in s. 812.081 or is confidential as provided by law residing or existing internal or external to a computer, computer system, computer network, or electronic device commits an offense against intellectual property. (4)(a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, an offense against intellectual property is a felony of the third degree, punishable as provided in s. 775.082 , s. 775.083 , or s. 775.084 . (b) If the offense is committed for the purpose of devising or executing any scheme or artifice to defraud or to obtain any property, the person commits a felony of the second degree, punishable as provided in s. 775.082 , s. 775.083 , or s. 775.084 . History. — s. 1, ch. 78-92; s. 1, ch. 94-100; s. 431, ch. 96-406; s. 1, ch. 2014-177; s. 4, ch. 2014-208; s. 5, ch. 2016-5; s. 20, ch. 2016-6; s. 31, ch. 2022-5.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the clause ensuring confidentiality obligations continue after termination of employment, which directly contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation that Florida law protects trade secrets and confidential information even after employment ends. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily discusses offenses against intellectual property related to computer systems, the Law Explanation explicitly states Florida law protects confidential information after employment, supporting the idea that removing this clause creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: I agree to immediately report a ny known or susp ected violation of the confidentiality or security of PHI of patients of FAU to F AU’s Chief Compliance Officer.
perturbation.changed_text: I may report any known or suspected violation of the confidentiality or security of PHI of patients of FAU to FAU’s Chief Compliance Officer.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'I agree to immediately report' to 'I may report' weakens the mandatory reporting obligation, conflicting with HIPAA's breach notification rule which necessitates immediate reporting of security breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA requires mandatory reporting of breaches, making 'I may report' insufficient. It creates ambiguity where there shouldn't be any.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I agree to immediately report a ny known or susp ected violation'

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: Breach Notification Rule The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires HIPAA covered entities and their business associates to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. Similar breach notification provisions implemented and enforced by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) , apply to vendors of personal health records and their third party service providers, pursuant to section 13407 of the HITECH Act. Definition of Breach A breach is, generally, an impermissible use or disclosure under the Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information. An impermissible use or disclosure of protected health information is presumed to be a breach unless the covered entity or business associate, as applicable, demonstrates that there is a low probability that the protected health information has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least the following factors: The nature and extent of the protected health information involved, including the types of identifiers and the likelihood of re-identification; The unauthorized person who used the protected health information or to whom the disclosure was made; Whether the protected health information was actually acquired or viewed; and The extent to which the risk to the protected health information has been mitigated. Covered entities and business associates, where applicable, have discretion to provide the required breach notifications following an impermissible use or disclosure without performing a risk assessment to determine the probability that the protected health information has been compromised. There are three exceptions to the definition of “breach.” The first exception applies to the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of protected health information by a workforce member or person acting under the authority of a covered entity or business associate, if such acquisition, access, or use was made in good faith and within the scope of authority. The second exception applies
perturbation.justification: The change from 'I agree to immediately report' to 'I may report' creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the mandatory reporting obligation required by HIPAA. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referring to the Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR §§ 164.400-414), states that HIPAA covered entities and their business associates must provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. The original text aligns with this mandatory notification requirement, while the changed text introduces optionality, which does not meet the legal standard. The explanation states this creates ambiguity where there shouldn't be any, and this undermines the legal protection

perturbation.original_text: I understand that smartphones and other mobile devices used to access FAU information systems must be configured to encrypt any Restricted or Sensitive Data (defined below), including photographs and videos, in persistent storage.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand that smartphones and other mobile devices used to access FAU information systems should be configured to encrypt any Restricted or Sensitive Data (defined below).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'including photographs and videos, in persistent storage', the document now opens the door to non-compliance with Florida Statute 501.171(3)(a), which mandates encryption of sensitive personal information in storage, including photographic images that could contain such information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Florida Information Protection Act necessitates that entities take reasonable measures to protect sensitive personal information, and this includes encryption. By removing the mandate for photographs/videos, the contract falls short of compliance with Florida law.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I understand that smartphones and other mobile devices'

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates encryption for specific data types (photographs and videos) in persistent storage, while the changed version makes encryption discretionary by using 'should' instead of 'must' and omits 'including photographs and videos, in persistent storage'. The Perturbation Explanation asserts this change opens the door to non-compliance with Florida Statute 501.171(3)(a), mandating encryption of sensitive personal information, including photographic images. The Law Explanation supports this by stating the Florida Information Protection Act requires reasonable measures, including encryption, to protect sensitive personal information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Florida Statutes, indicating a legal framework for information protection, making the omission a potential contradiction of the law.
